title: RA.973 Kia
tracklist:

translation.content: While it might feel early to call bets on DJ players of the decade, we're confident Kia Sydney, best known simply as Kia, is one of them. The Animalia founder began her journey in Melbourne's scene in the mid-2010s, crediting a trip to the influential Japanese deep techno festival, Labyrinth, as the inspiration behind her sound. Deep techno might not cut it as a descriptor for Sydney's style, though. Sure, you'll hear hypnotic ribbons of steely techno, but that oomph and edge is also softened with atmospherics and nimble grooves that hew closer to IDM, dub and tech house. It's a sound that shares as much with the likes of DJ Nobu and Donato Dozzy (try to find the track that overlaps with Dozzy's own RA Podcast) as it does with modern practitioners like Priori and Beatrice M. Nowadays, this distinctly Australian brand of scuttling psychedelia has made Kia among the most sought-after underground DJs across the globe. It's also due in large part to Sydney's brainchild, Animalia, which showcases a plurality of sounds from Vlada and Konduku to Melbourne locals like Reptant and Zara. The hybrid label, mix and event series is living proof that the 2020s mentality-shift has been fruitful: less serious, but with a sense of open-minded worldliness that offers a more promising vision of what dance music can be and achieve. Sydney's talent lies, most explicitly, in forging connections. With a distinct playfulness, Sydney brings people, sounds, ideas together—and her RA Podcast showcases this alchemy in abundance. As she notes in her interview, RA.973 is akin to a "signature," connecting classics from Monolake to Enya to Sydney's peers, such as OK EG, Cousin and Command D. In our 2023 Breaking Through profile, Sydney explained, "people tell me I have quite a distinctive sound but I can't tell so much because I hear so many different versions of it." RA.973 is an exercise in wicked mixing, with an ear for unexpected textures and blends few others could pull off—a firm confirmation that Kia's kaleidoscopic style is the mark of a generational talent. - Bella Aquilina What have you been working on recently? I've recently moved back home to Melbourne—I'm trying to balance my touring life with my regular life a bit more in the new year, so I'm using this time to reset, reflect, process the insane few years I've had and plan ahead. Rather than living in Europe, I've decided to make the most of my incredible home and community here with shorter tours overseas. My real passion lies in curating, and at the moment I'm focusing on this. I'm really grateful to do this in various capacities within the Melbourne scene: running regular Animalia events at my favourite community music venue, Miscellania, a few Animalia releases in the works and some ambient releases on Cirrus, my downtempo output. I'm also working on officially launching un:send, a festival I run with my friends Kasun (who runs Temporal Cast), Dylan (AKA Paper-Cuts), Matt Wilson and Lauren Squire (AKA OK EG). Stay tuned. Can you tell us about how and where your mix was recorded, and the concept behind it? At a friend's studio in Melbourne on a Xone:96 and four CDJs. I really wanted to record it at home, but my own set-up isn't quite ready yet, and I needed some extra CDJs for this one. I wanted the mix to represent the various sides of me, since I see the RA Podcast series as a bit of a "signature." It's a blend of sentimental nostalgia, exciting current and forthcoming things. There's some self-indulgent nods to special memories (Lockgroove's "Feel the Joy"), my favourite deep house song that I played at Freerotation this year and Two Full Minds' "No Smoke (Deep Dap Dap)," as well as an Enya moment—an ode to my melancholic moments of solitude during touring. There's also some new stuff I'm loving playing at the moment, as well as forthcoming bits from Animalia and Cirrus. I wanted to represent plenty of homegrown talent, so there's music from OK EG, Cousin and Louis Marlo. It's not perfect, but it's very me—playful, spontaneous and sometimes a bit spaced out. Is there a social or political cause you feel deserves more global attention? Having been fortunate to grow up in Australia, it's important to acknowledge that while life here can be wonderful for many, Australia is a colony with a shameful past. Our First Nations people remain among the most disadvantaged communities globally. I hope the much-needed focus on the situation in Gaza serves as a broader reminder of the realities of imperialism. We need to foster greater empathy and awareness of the shared struggles faced by Indigenous peoples worldwide—starting, most importantly, with those here at home. I feel humbled by their support and solidarity towards one another, as I've seen some of the loudest voices for Palestine here at home from Indigenous leaders and friends. We all have so much to learn from them and we should be paying more attention. I definitely could, and should be doing much more for this cause. It's a goal for 2025. Which club or party has had the most significant influence on your growth as an artist? I can narrow it down to two. I attended Inner Varnika for eight years, from about the age of 18 until the finale in 2023. Every year, I saw many talented locals and internationals, representing a wide array of styles. It had a big impact on my passion for electronic music. It was there that I first found the drive to learn to DJ—especially seeing the early wave of Australian female DJs play there. I remember being so young, watching Millu, Pjenne, Laila Saikini and Toni Yotzi play there and, realising "oh cool, there's amazing women doing it here too, not just men, I’m going to give it a shot." Het Weekend at De School would be the other one. I was 23 and it was one of my first gigs in Europe—it had a very long-lasting impact on me and I was lucky enough to continue playing there until it closed [last year]. I experienced nothing short of pure bliss and euphoria on multiple occasions on the dance floor of Het Muzieklokaal. Animalia has become a vital platform for Australian and emerging artists, evolving into a label, mix and event series. How have you seen the scene shift since you started in 2020? Tradition, gate-keeping attitudes and purism are being rewritten and replaced by a wider spectrum—not just styles of music, but also diversity, representation and attitudes in the scene. It's long overdue. I'm happy Animalia has been part of that wave, as it's become a diverse and wide-reaching platform. The scene is becoming more open-minded, accessible and flexible and I think that’s never a bad thing. Music and the arts have always been political, but it's been empowering to realise I'm part of a community that increasingly has the ability to push for change and stand-up for the things we believe in. Since the pandemic, you've been touring consistently around the world. What have the primary challenges of the experience been? What I've learnt is that the imposter syndrome and questions of identity never really go away. Success is subjective and there's no real measure for it. What's been very humbling is meeting so many of my idols and realising they feel exactly the same way I do, struggling with pressure and confidence at times. If you had to choose one artist or release that you feel best encapsulates your style, who or what would it be? I wouldn't call our styles similar, but I'll say Shackleton (and his entire Skull Disco catalogue). His music feels deeply personal and almost self-indulgent, which I relate to—it's also weird, often outrageous, but equally playful and exciting music.
translation.blurb: Kaleidoscopic psychedelia from one of Australia's finest.
